content.text: An open source software (OSS) usage policy for GenAI applications defines the rules governing which OSS licenses are permitted in AI-related codebases, which require legal review before use, and which are prohibited without an approved exception. For GenAI applications, the policy must cover not only traditional software libraries and frameworks but also AI model weights, training datasets, and fine-tuning base models—each of which carries its own licensing obligations that may differ from standard software license analysis.

The policy is distinct from a general software OSS policy in one critical respect: many AI models and datasets use custom or AI-specific licenses (the Meta Llama Community License, Google's Gemma Terms of Use, Stability AI's RAIL licenses) that do not map cleanly onto the established open source license taxonomy. A GenAI-specific policy must address these licenses explicitly rather than assuming the traditional permissive/copyleft classification applies.

OSS licensing risk in GenAI applications is higher than in traditional software for several reasons. GenAI applications often incorporate large numbers of OSS libraries in complex dependency trees where copyleft licenses can be hidden several layers deep. The AI models used in GenAI applications may carry licensing terms that restrict commercial use, prohibit specific applications, or impose use-case conditions that must be honored. Training datasets used to fine-tune models may carry data-specific licensing obligations — CC licenses, database rights, or bespoke terms — that flow through to the trained model.

Organizations that deploy GenAI applications without a clear OSS usage policy risk AGPL copyleft contamination that could require open-sourcing their proprietary application code, commercial-use restrictions in model licenses that make the deployment unlawful, and data licensing violations that create copyright infringement claims from dataset contributors.
Prevents AGPL and GPL copyleft contamination that could require open-sourcing proprietary GenAI application code
Creates an approved-license register that engineers can consult without engaging Legal for each dependency decision
Ensures compliance with AI-specific model licenses that restrict commercial use, geographic deployment, or application type
Provides the foundation for SBOM (Software Bill of Materials) generation required by increasing numbers of enterprise customers and by regulation such as the US Executive Order on Cybersecurity
Reduces due diligence burden in M&A and customer security questionnaire responses by having a documented policy
Establishes accountability for license compliance through a named approval workflow for restricted licenses
A policy that permits only permissive licenses minimizes risk but blocks useful LGPL and MPL-licensed libraries; a tiered approach requires more governance overhead but enables more OSS components
Automated license scanning with pre-approved lists scales well but misses edge cases and AI-specific licenses that do not appear in standard license databases
AI model licenses change with each version; a model licensed permissively in version 1 may have commercial-use restrictions added in version 2 without the consuming organization noticing
If a fine-tuned model is based on a dataset with share-alike license terms, the training outputs may be subject to the same license, creating obligations that affect how the model can be deployed
An OSS usage policy for GenAI applications defines three tiers of license classification, an internal register of approved components, and an approval workflow for non-standard licenses.

The approved tier covers licenses that impose only minimal obligations — attribution and license text inclusion — without any copyleft requirements. Engineers can use components under these licenses without seeking additional approval. The list typically includes MIT, Apache 2.0, BSD 2-Clause and 3-Clause, ISC, and CC0 1.0 for datasets. Apache 2.0 is preferred over MIT for software libraries because it includes an explicit patent grant.
Tier 2: Review Required (Weak Copyleft and Dataset Licenses)

The review tier covers licenses with specific obligations that must be evaluated in the context of the planned use. LGPL libraries may be usable if dynamically linked but trigger copyleft obligations if statically linked. MPL 2.0 applies copyleft at the file level rather than the project level. CC BY and CC BY-SA apply to datasets and documentation and may impose share-alike obligations on derivative works including models fine-tuned on those datasets. Use of components in this tier requires Legal sign-off before incorporation.
Tier 3: Prohibited (Strong Copyleft and Commercial Restrictions)

The prohibited tier covers licenses that impose obligations incompatible with most commercial GenAI deployments. GPL 2.0 and 3.0 require derivative works to be distributed under the GPL, which would require open-sourcing proprietary application code. AGPL 3.0 extends copyleft to network use — software running as a service — making it particularly dangerous for GenAI API services. SSPL 1.0 (used by MongoDB) and BUSL 1.1 impose commercial restrictions. Components in this tier cannot be used without an explicit exception approved by the CISO and Legal.

AI model licenses require separate analysis because they do not map onto the traditional OSS license taxonomy. The Meta Llama Community License permits commercial use for most organizations but restricts use by companies with more than 700 million monthly active users. Google's Gemma Terms of Use permit commercial use but prohibit certain applications including use for weapons, surveillance, or content that violates Google's usage policies. Stability AI's RAIL (Responsible AI License) adds use-case restrictions to an otherwise permissive base. Each AI model license must be reviewed individually against the specific deployment context.

The policy requires maintaining an internal register of all OSS components and AI models in use, with their license classifications. New components require an approval workflow: Tier 1 components can be added by any engineer with a record in the register; Tier 2 components require Legal sign-off documented in the register; Tier 3 components require CISO and Legal co-approval and a documented exception rationale.

attrs.alt: OSS License Classification for GenAI Applications

content.text: The diagram shows the three-tier license classification with representative licenses and their key obligations in each tier. The bottom note highlights that AI-specific model licenses such as the Llama Community License and Gemma Terms of Use require separate analysis beyond the standard tiered classification.
During the initial selection of an AI model or framework for a new GenAI application before any code is written
When adding a new dependency to an existing GenAI application, at the point of the pull request that introduces it
When upgrading an existing dependency to a new version that may have changed its license
When fine-tuning a model on a new dataset, to assess the dataset's licensing obligations and how they affect the trained model
During due diligence for M&A or partnership, to assess the target's OSS license compliance posture in their GenAI assets
When generating an SBOM for a customer or regulatory requirement

Draft the three-tier classification and the AI-model-specific license review requirement. Identify the current most-used AI models and frameworks in use at the organization and add their license classifications to the register. Have Legal review the tier classifications against the organization's specific deployment model — SaaS vs. on-premises vs. embedded — as the same license can have different implications in each context.

Create a structured register with fields for: component name, version, license SPDX identifier, tier classification, use context, approval status, Legal sign-off reference (for Tier 2), and exception reference (for Tier 3). The register should be version-controlled alongside the codebase.
Step 3: Integrate with the development workflow

Integrate OSS license checking into the CI/CD pipeline using a license scanning tool. Configure the scanner to flag Tier 2 components for review and block Tier 3 components from merging without an exception documented in the register. The scanner should also flag new or unknown licenses for manual classification.
Step 4: Establish the AI model review process

Define a process for reviewing AI-specific model licenses. For each base model and training dataset used, create a review record documenting the license terms, the planned use case, and Legal's assessment of compliance. Update the review when upgrading to a new model version.

# OSS License Policy — Approved License Register (excerpt)
# Format: SPDX ID | Tier | Common Use | Key Obligation | Notes

licenses:
  MIT:
    tier: 1_approved
    spdx_id: MIT
    common_use: General software libraries
    obligation: Include copyright notice and license text
    notes: Preferred for minimal-obligation use cases

  Apache-2.0:
    tier: 1_approved
    spdx_id: Apache-2.0
    common_use: General software libraries, cloud tools
    obligation: Include NOTICE file and license text; patent retaliation clause
    notes: Preferred over MIT for projects where patent protection matters

  LGPL-3.0-only:
    tier: 2_review_required
    spdx_id: LGPL-3.0-only
    common_use: Dynamically linked libraries
    obligation: Dynamic linking: OK. Static linking or modification: must release LGPL source.
    approval_required: Legal sign-off
    notes: Verify linking mode before approval; document in exception register

  GPL-3.0-only:
    tier: 3_prohibited
    spdx_id: GPL-3.0-only
    common_use: Many system tools, some ML libraries
    obligation: All derivative works must be GPL-licensed and source-disclosed
    approval_required: CISO + Legal exception
    notes: Incompatible with proprietary code distribution

  AGPL-3.0-only:
    tier: 3_prohibited
    spdx_id: AGPL-3.0-only
    common_use: Some database and ML libraries (e.g., older Langchain versions)
    obligation: Copyleft extends to network use (SaaS); all application code linked
                to AGPL must be disclosed to users
    approval_required: CISO + Legal exception
    notes: Particularly dangerous for GenAI API services

ai_model_licenses:
  llama-3-community-license:
    tier: 2_review_required
    applies_to: Meta Llama 3.x model weights
    commercial_use: Permitted for <700M MAU organizations
    prohibited_uses: Weapons, surveillance, deception, violations of Meta policies
    approval_required: Legal sign-off on use case
    derivative_model_naming: Models based on Llama must include "Llama" in name

  gemma-terms-of-use:
    tier: 2_review_required
    applies_to: Google Gemma model weights
    commercial_use: Permitted
    prohibited_uses: Per Google Prohibited Use Policy
    approval_required: Legal sign-off on use case

  cc-by-sa-4.0:
    tier: 2_review_required
    applies_to: Datasets and training data
    obligation: Share-alike: derivative works including fine-tuned models
                may be subject to CC BY-SA if the dataset is a substantial
                part of the training data
    approval_required: Legal sign-off on training data use

A startup built a GenAI coding assistant that incorporated an AGPL-licensed code analysis library they found on GitHub. The library was a transitive dependency of a popular Python package, not a direct import. When preparing for a Series B due diligence, the legal team's OSS review discovered the AGPL dependency. Because the application was served as a SaaS API, the AGPL's network-use copyleft clause applied, potentially requiring the company to open-source their entire application codebase under the AGPL.

The company spent three weeks replacing the AGPL library with an Apache 2.0-licensed alternative before the due diligence concluded. Had the OSS usage policy been in place from the start with CI/CD scanning, the AGPL dependency would have been flagged at the pull request stage and would never have entered the codebase.
The AGPL library that causes copyleft contamination is rarely a direct import; it is three levels deep in the dependency tree
Treating AI model licenses as standard OSS licenses:
Many AI model licenses impose use-case restrictions that do not appear in traditional OSS license analysis tools
Not updating license reviews when upgrading model versions:
A model that was licensed permissively in version 1 may have commercial-use restrictions added in version 2
Training data licenses can impose obligations on the trained model and on derivative fine-tuned models
A policy that relies on engineers manually checking licenses before adding dependencies is not enforced at the point of use
Without a formal exception pathway, prohibited components either block legitimate use cases or get used anyway without documented review
Scan all dependencies including transitive ones, not just direct imports
Integrate license scanning into CI/CD so that prohibited components are blocked at the pull request level, not discovered post-deployment
Maintain a separate AI model license register that captures use-case restrictions and derivative work obligations for each base model
Review AI model license terms every time a new model version is adopted, not just at initial adoption
Include dataset license analysis in the review of any fine-tuning project
Define an exception process with named approvers so that legitimate use cases involving restricted licenses can proceed with documented review
Generate an SBOM on every release so that license compliance evidence is available for customer due diligence and regulatory requirements

License scanning at scale requires automation. Large GenAI codebases with hundreds of direct and thousands of transitive dependencies cannot be managed with manual review. Commercial tools such as FOSSA, Black Duck, and Snyk integrate with CI/CD pipelines and provide policy-as-code enforcement where approved licenses are automatically allowed, review-required licenses generate a warning, and prohibited licenses block the merge.

The AI model license register is harder to automate because AI-specific licenses are not in standard SPDX databases and their conditions are use-case-specific rather than technically deterministic. Budget for at least one Legal or licensing-specialist review per quarter to keep the AI model register current as new models and license versions are released.
Commercial OSS compliance platform with CI/CD integration, policy enforcement, SBOM generation, and support for custom license definitions including AI-specific licenses
Enterprise-grade OSS scanning with comprehensive vulnerability and license databases; strong on transitive dependency detection
License compliance and vulnerability scanning integrated into developer workflows with IDE plugins and CI/CD gates
Lightweight Python and Node.js CLI tools for generating local dependency license reports; suitable for pre-commit hooks
SBOM format standards; use CycloneDX for AI-specific SBOM extensions that cover model metadata and training data provenance
Linux Foundation REUSE tooling for annotating source files with SPDX license identifiers, making license detection more reliable

OSS license policy enforcement is a security control in the sense that unexpected licenses in the codebase represent an unresolved legal risk that can materialize as business disruption. Ensuring the policy is enforced in CI/CD rather than only in periodic audits means the control operates continuously rather than at point-in-time snapshots.

The OSS component register is an asset that must be maintained with the same diligence as the codebase itself. Stale registers that do not reflect current dependency trees give a false sense of compliance. Regenerate the SBOM and validate against the register on every release.
GenAI OSS policy must cover three categories beyond traditional libraries: AI model weights, training datasets, and AI-specific licenses that do not appear in standard license databases
AGPL 3.0 is the most dangerous license for SaaS GenAI products because its copyleft extends to network use — software running as a service
AI-specific model licenses (Llama Community License, Gemma Terms of Use, RAIL licenses) require separate per-model legal analysis beyond the standard tier classification
Transitive dependency scanning is mandatory; the copyleft library that causes contamination is rarely a direct import
Dataset licenses can impose obligations on fine-tuned models; include dataset license analysis in all fine-tuning projects
Integrate license scanning into CI/CD as a blocking gate for prohibited licenses, not only as a periodic audit
Generate an SBOM on every release for customer due diligence and regulatory compliance
